subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: governance/politics rebuilt from independent/multilateral sources (IMF ECF/RSF reviews, World Bank MPO, UN/OCHA/UNHCR, V-Dem, Freedom House 2026, RSF 2026, HRW, ACLED, Crisis Group) + international press (Reuters/AFP/Al Jazeera); junta/state media EXCLUDED on governance, rights, and security claims, and online pro-Traore narratives treated as non-evidential.

Data limitation: a data-poor active-conflict state with the worst territorial control of the three; security/casualty and IDP figures are external estimates with reduced confidence; the CFA franc is euro-pegged via BCEAO with a French convertibility guarantee.

Standout drags: the deadliest jihadist war in the Sahel (~40%+ of territory contested, ~2M+ IDPs, sharply rising civilian killings), a Traore junta that postponed elections indefinitely with a personalist consolidation and disappearances, and a Russia pivot; the IMF-anchored gold economy with low inflation is the rare stabilizer. Net: clearly negative with a downside tail.

economic.notes: A surprisingly resilient gold/CFA economy with low inflation and an on-track IMF program, undercut by resource nationalization and the war.

fiscal_state.notes: Growth ~5% with improved revenue and a moderate (sustainable) debt-distress risk under an IMF ECF arrangement (4th review Feb-2026), though security spending dominates. (IMF ECF 2026; World Bank MPO)

monetary_independence.notes: The CFA-franc euro peg gives stability and a French convertibility guarantee but zero autonomy, under the regional BCEAO. (IMF/BCEAO)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The CFA franc is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Low and peg-anchored - near zero/negative in 2025 on falling food prices, converging to ~2% - a structural strength. (IMF; World Bank MPO)

banking_stability.notes: BCEAO-supervised and peg-backed, but exposed to sovereign borrowing and the conflict economy. (IMF)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Gold (~75% of exports, output up to ~94t in 2025) and cotton, but the state nationalized five mines into SOPAMIB under a revised code, raising investor risk. (Mining.com; Reuters; State Dept ICS)

institutional.notes: A personalist coup junta ruling by decree with indefinitely postponed elections and severe press repression.

rule_of_law.notes: Military rule since the 2022 coups; Traore granted a 5-year renewable term, elections postponed indefinitely, with abductions and forced conscription of critics. (Freedom House 2026; HRW; Amnesty)

statistical_integrity.notes: Still IMF-engaged via ECF reviews, but conflict-zone inaccessibility and security secrecy degrade reliability. (IMF)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Weak with the state absent from much of the territory, though revenue administration has improved. (World Bank; IMF)

press_freedom.notes: Among the worst declines - French outlets suspended, journalists abducted/conscripted, and calls to murder critics. (RSF 2026; HRW; FIDH; CPJ)

political_social.notes: The Sahel's worst displacement and civilian-killing trajectory under an indefinitely entrenched personalist junta.

political_stability.notes: A Traore cult-of-personality consolidation with foiled coup plots, but the state controls perhaps only ~60-70% of territory amid a worsening insurgency. (ICG; Africa Center; ACLED)

civil_liberties.notes: Severe - dissidents and journalists abducted or forcibly conscripted, protest banned, and a 5-point Freedom House drop. (Freedom House 2026; HRW; Amnesty)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts some regional refugees while generating mass displacement; protection severely funding-constrained. (UNHCR; OCHA)

social_cohesion.notes: Ethnic (Fulani-targeting) massacres by militias/forces and jihadists, with ~2M+ IDPs fracturing communities. (HRW; ACLED; OCHA)

demographics.notes: Very young and high-fertility, but war, displacement, and school closures gut the potential dividend. (UN; OCHA)

geopolitical.notes: Pivoted to Russia and out of ECOWAS into the AES, amid the region's deadliest insurgency.

alliance_reliability.notes: Exited ECOWAS (Jan-2025) for the Russia-aligned AES, expelled French forces, and turned to Russian support - isolated from the West. (Reuters; Amani Africa; observed)

conflict_involvement.notes: The Sahel's deadliest jihadist war (JNIM/ISSP), with ~40%+ of territory contested and a sharp rise in civilian deaths. (ACLED; ICG; State Dept)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No comprehensive Western sanctions, but mine nationalizations and the ECOWAS exit raise expropriation/capital risk. (Reuters; State Dept ICS)

physical_practical.notes: Acute insecurity over much of the country, atop severe climate stress, weak health, and damaged infrastructure.

climate.notes: Among the world's most vulnerable (ND-GAIN bottom-15) - Sahelian drought, desertification, heat, conflict-degraded readiness. (ND-GAIN)

healthcare.notes: Collapsed across conflict zones - hundreds of health facilities closed - amid acute malnutrition and aid cuts. (WHO/OCHA; ICRC)

infrastructure.notes: Landlocked with road networks contested by militants and large no-go areas; gold-belt logistics strained. (World Bank; ACLED)

crime_safety.notes: Among the world's least safe, with widespread massacres, blockaded towns, and abductions. (ACLED; HRW; advisories)

disaster_insurance.notes: Negligible penetration against extreme conflict and climate exposure. (observed)

summary: Burkina Faso, under Captain Ibrahim Traore since the 2022 coups, faces the Sahel's deadliest jihadist war: roughly 40% or more of the territory is contested, civilian killings have risen sharply, and some 2 million people are internally displaced. The junta runs a personalist consolidation - a five-year renewable term, indefinitely postponed elections, abducted or forcibly conscripted critics, suspended foreign media - while pivoting to Russia and exiting ECOWAS for the AES. The rare bright spot is the economy: an IMF ECF program is on track with ~5% growth and unusually low (near-zero) CFA-peg-anchored inflation, funded by gold (~75% of exports), though the nationalization of five mines into state-owned SOPAMIB raises investor risk. With external data and contested security figures, the assessment is clearly negative with a meaningful state-erosion tail.
